item.title: SCP 682 related?
item.content:encoded: I don't understand why 682 is 'related' to this SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EDIT — August 13: Looking back, I think my criticism here was way too harsh; I failed to take into consideration that this was the author's second article on the site from way back when. In any case, take the following thoughts with a grain of salt.

I have to say, every narrative step this article takes forward ends up working against it. Both the anomaly and its story have been buried and yielded incomprehensible by incessant redactions and confusing descriptions and chronology. Frankly, this article has a basic misunderstanding of what it wants to be.

+ Feedback
– hide block

I have plenty of criticisms, but I will try to ignore any that stem from "sign of the times" errors (meaning: errors stemming from the lower standards this article was released under). It doesn't mean I haven't taken those kinds of errors into consideration when voting.

Special Containment Procedures

All samples delivered are in containment.

I'm honestly confused right out of the gate. Samples of what, and where have they been delivered to? What tangible information does this provide except stating "the parts of the anomaly that we contained are contained"?

The distance of █████ from Earth and the conditions on the planet itself render any breach of containment of active instances of SCP-1124 unlikely

I won't criticize this for the use of a redaction in the containment (actually, I will, but it's neither here or there because I don't like any of the redactions in this article). What I will criticize is, it states that disseminating information about the distance and properties won't breach containment, but then fails to provide that information anyway. Why even point that out? And how will researchers know where this thing is if they don't have that info? Is there a risk of an internal Foundation security breach? Then you'd have to call into question why the Foundation is so poor at its containment management.

Description

The first instances of SCP-1124 were recovered from canisters

Were they all found inside the canisters, or only the ones found under the mountain? Is ILYENA next to this mountain? In any case, why is it important that only the first were discovered under the mountain?

weighing exactly 1.0268 kg.

This is very precise, and as such, is mostly unnecessary.

which will cause instances of SCP-1124 to activate and [DATA EXPUNGED].

All this communicates is "it will activate and do something so bad that we can't even say".

Though observations are performed daily through a viewing window in tank 1124-C by monitoring cameras, a definite shape cannot be substantiated.

Wasn't it previously stated that they look like cephalopods?

SCP-1124-C is in all instances metallic in appearance.

Wasn't it already stated that they are all metallic?

The entity frequently gnaws and consumes the casing in its containment unit, although the amount of damage taken is acceptable due to the density and thinness of the film used to line the chamber.

Three things here:

  1. What happened to the canisters? If the canisters contained these things under a mountain for an unspecified amount of time, then why have they been transferred out of them?
  2. Why would the researchers use inadequate containment materials here?
  3. Why is it considered acceptable that the entity regularly eats at these inadequate containment materials?

Further research into containment is deemed necessary as the creature has been shown to grow in minor increments when more material is consumed.

This only increasingly fails to justify why such unacceptable levels of containment are being met: they are essentially allowing these things to be well-fed and continue to grow.

A tear located in Researcher █████████'s body suit during transfer allowed [DATA EXPUNGED] activating 1 (one) instance of SCP-1124.

So you're saying that [DATA EXPUNGED] leads to activation, which then leads to [DATA EXPUNGED]? Also, why was the researcher so careless as to wear a torn body suit when transferring this thing?

and then launching itself into the mouth of one Researcher █████████.

Wait, there's another researcher in the same room without any protections whatsoever?!

This method of mobility is believed to be a spring like motion using its appendages but this is still unconfirmed.

Surely all they would have to do is check the videotapes to verify this?

Following containment procedures Researcher █████████ sealed himself inside unit 1124-C and activated the failsafe mechanism

This is an example of what caused the aforementioned confusion in my opening spiel. I cannot notice the difference between the redaction lengths, and thus can't actually differentiate how many researchers are helping contain it. Also, which researcher was attacked: the one with the tear in their suit or the one that had the entity enter its body via open mouth? The latter would make sense, but it's left ambiguous.

x-23c ILYENA [6555-x23] Lander: Transmitted Data:

Is this before or after the previously-described incident?

07:27:30:

07:28:23:
Sanders: Couldn’t sneeze so I just spat on one. They don’t seem to be affected. Think they might just be figurines.

07:28:56:
Sanders: Mother mercy one of those appendages is actually moving! Woo boy that scared the sh-

01:00:00:
Indecipherable noise

This is just egregious lolFoundation. Also, isn't Sanders one of the lead researchers on these things? Why are they being so irresponsible?

And then it breached containment for plot to happen. That's two separate occasions of researchers acting incredibly irresponsible when handling the entities, without anything learned in-between.

Video Recording 1124C area:

And this is just a major chronology and pacing issue. If this is the incident from before, then why is it only being described right now? The difference between this incident and the one recorded from the lander is imprecise and only muddles the chronology further.

Short History of ILYENA, Project Tantalus, and the Grey Dragon Incident

The reveal of its relation to SCP-682 just kills this for me completely.

Ultimately, this is not a good article and is really showing its age.

item.title: hmm.....
item.content:encoded: aren't these thins a little similar to facehuggers from the alien movies?

item.title: What?
item.content:encoded:
I honestly have no idea what's happening from beginning to end, and I'm not even sure what the SCP IS. Firstly, telling me about the discovery AFTER the addendum,and then telling me about -C BEFORE telling me what it is, is extremely confusing. Secondly, I'm really unsure what SCP-682 has to do with any of this really, or why it's blacked out in the first place. Hell, I don't even know where the SCPs are at this point. Earth? Io? Alpha Centauri? Do they transform the things that touch them, or are they all tiny robot killing machines?
I may just have low reading comprehension, but until I know what the hell this article says, I'll have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way too over the top. Destroying a continent (Antartica according to the author) is way too much for me. I don't really mind it being set in the future, and also in my headcanon the Foundation has technology more advanced than the rest of the world. I don't believe the 682 egg part truly fits my headcanon, since 682 is a son of the Scarlet King, brother of 999.
It is well written though, so the 8th passanger gets a no vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What they should do is use fluoroantimonic acid instead, that stuff has a ph of -31. And while they’re at it use it as the new acid for scp-682 as well

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t understand why the designation of 682 is blacked out throughout the entire thing but then it’s made obvious what it is at the bottom by the related scp objects

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap, I love this.

Also, that audio file was… Just take my upvote.

item.title: Regarding the recent page revision
item.content:encoded: Just an FYI, I went ahead and changed the audio player from Flash to HTML5, as Flash is rapidly losing support and already can't be viewed by many people, including Firefox and Mobile users. No content changes were made, and the article is otherwise unchanged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Faminepulse, I usually either love or hate your skips. This one errs into the latter category.

See, you had an amazing hook in your SCProcedures. But then you led me into a nigh-incomprehensible description, which pretty much destroyed my immersion and made me unable to invest myself in the rest of the skip.

-1

item.title: Re: Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: I distinctly remember Famine making a post about removing an image due to being unable to find it. Although, it is possible I misremebered that.

item.title: Re: Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Well we don’t know if that’s why he did it but the reversion seems right to respect Famine’s edit.

item.title: Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded:
I have reverted due to the reediting of pictures back into the article, which faminepulse (the author) specifically removed due to being unable to find the source.

I reverted to edit 47, if anyone is curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why, but this is incredibly scary.

1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, these things are clerly not organic. They adapt and grow somewhat. 682 is organic and can adapt better than them. And grow really fast. Maybe they are the counter to him a civilization made, but failed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The other possibility is that these things are why 682 exists- a desperate countermeasure that works all too well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does the link at the end of the piece mean that the "682 eggs" thing is now canon? I mean, it seems to imply that, but I'm hoping I'm reading too much into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that a thing? Internet research only results in bikini pictures…

Specifically regarding nukes, "Fail-Deadly" would be the term. That encompasses both intentional ("Dead Hand") and unintentional (Chernobyl) nuclear excitement precipitated by control being in some way compromised.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's really all there is to see on the second image. The other two, right click and hit "view image"- you can zoom in on an image from there. Might also up the gamma setting and lower the contrast on your monitor- low gamma and high contrast will make anything look like a black box with lines on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to like the article, but it needs a bit of cleaning and I want to see a brightened and clearer version of the pictures as I see only a black box with lines on it, my eyesight is awful after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy fuck that audio clip

+1 man

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps you mean a fail-hot nuclear device?

Is that a thing? Internet research only results in bikini pictures…

At any rate, I think that "failsafe" is correct here. The failsafe in this case is not on the device, but rather the device is a failsafe for the containment procedures. A "failsafe" minimizes damage in the event of a failure. In this case, if containment fails, and the failsafe detonates, it does so 70km underground.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the third paragraph of the procedures, you mention a "failsafe nuclear device". Usually "failsafe" means it doesn't explode when something goes wrong. Perhaps you mean a fail-hot nuclear device?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to upvote but then I read the discussion page. I simply don't care for us being the last sentient life in the universe. Doesn't at all fit with my headcanon and I like my headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey… isn't this Prometheus? :P

[Fun thing is it's similar in the same way that 173 is similar to the Weeping Angels — eerily close, but the SCP was published first. :P ]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
4) In the last paragraph under the last collapsible: "Project Tantalus was began…" NO. That's just bad writing and utterly inexcusable.

Those are called typos. They can happen.

Furthermore, why would the Foundation be in the year 22XX and still be in the No. 1100's?

Just noting that's a headcanon nitpick. I would find it believable, if the foundation found a new anomaly every year or so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Show comment
hide

Okay, before I even read the comments section, I had several major gripes with this article:

1) Too Much Information. Yes, some SCP articles can get away with being long, but when I open a collapsable, see about five more paragraphs, and literally fall back in my seat with exasperation and say "Oh god, there's more?!", something's not right. The Special Containment Procedures in particular were too many words with too little meaning. I barely understood what I was reading in both the SCP and Description sections. I'd give advice for trimming it down, but I'm not an approved SCP-writer myself; I just know it was tiring to read.

2) The article talks about some of the results of SCP-1124-C, then tells the story of what exactly SCP-1124-C even is a paragraph or two later. That's just confusing and contrary to quality standards.

3) Blackboxes and expungements took away everything that could have helped me keep track of what I was even reading. My eyes started just skipping over the blackboxes, and I'd have to read sentences five times to even get the gist of what was being said. Also, "[INFORMATION OMITTED]"? We have "[REDACTED]" and "[DATA EXPUNGED]" already, since when is this new one a thing? Though that could just as easily be a plus as a minus.

4) In the last paragraph under the last collapsible: "Project Tantalus was began…" NO. That's just bad writing and utterly inexcusable.

The only good thing I had to say about this was that I learned that "vincible" is actually a word, and that had nothing to do with the content of the article whatsoever. That, and it (at the time) appeared to earn my SCP Nazi seal of approval in that it's a thing the Foundation needs to contain in order to protect the common understanding of "normal" and "possible". This was between novote and downvote in my head upon finishing the article itself, and that's rare for something that can pass my SCP Nazi litmus test.

Then I came and read the comments section, and seeing a lot of people make similar and also completely unrelated complaints, I now have a whole host of new things to gripe about, though admittedly all of them can be considered subcategories of the one huge gripe: the whole intergalactic mess and the year-22XX stuff. Not only does that explain a lot of things I couldn't follow about the article, it makes this absolutely terrible. If human technology can proceed to a point where we can travel to places in other galaxies and a whole continent has been destroyed here on Earth, these things ought to be explainable by what would, under those circumstances, be modern science (see, now that I get the article, it doesn't really even pass the SCP Nazi litmus test anymore). Furthermore, why would the Foundation be in the year 22XX and still be in the No. 1100's? We've had SCP objects from the future with notes denoting them as I think number six-or-so-digits, let alone even something as low as in the 6000's, so what's with SCP-1124 being from the distant future? And yes, I know the author begrudgingly went back and changed everything to year 20XX, but that just makes the content of the article significantly more confusing than it already was.

I am not against the 682-brethren idea. Honestly, I have no opinion on that at all. Whether these things are that or not, this article was hard to follow, uninteresting, exasperating, and now that I know the thoughts and such behind it, an insult to this site (in my opinion, for what little it's worth - I know full well I'm nobody, I'm just expressing my personal feelings). Articles must make sense both in-universe and out-of-universe to really be considered quality articles, and a lot of things about this don't make sense from either angle. There are a few endearing qualities, such as the Foundation personnel needing to be willingly murdered in the event of a containment breach, but it's nowhere near enough to carry an article, especially an article as long, confusing, and downright messed-up as this one. Downvoted hard.

Seriously, wall of text there. Trim it down or collapse it next time. ~Zyn

item.title: Re: What if...?
item.content:encoded:
I think Moose posted this mostly in relation to the 2000 contest. I don't think it was posted only because he personally thought it was good.

This is incorrect. What actually happened is that I was going to feature it later on. I bumped it up the schedule because of the timing of the 2000 contest — otherwise you'd be seeing this featured months from now, due to a number of behind-the-scenes specifications we have for featured articles (some of which are in the Ask A Senior Staff thread, some of which are secret, some of which are arbitrary).

But as I said earlier, I think this is the kind of thing that would work really well for the contest, so it got bumped up so people could see it while they were writing their drafts.

I do personally think this is a good article. Minor flaws in writing do not prevent something from being featured. We would not feature an article that we didn't think was both exceptional (not necessarily perfect, but exceptional) and under-appreciated.

[Note also that I/we don't choose to feature articles in a vacuum; others liked this article as well, and in fact it was suggested by Crayne & supported by a number of others.]

Personally, the issue that bugs me most is actually just that the Related Documentation collapsible is so easy to miss and I'm not sure it needs to be collapsed.

I think the fact that the reader can fill in the blanks is a plus to the writing of the article.

And the flavor text is not any kind of canon to the article. It wasn't created with input from Faminepulse. (This is par for the course.)

item.title: Re: My second article...
item.content:encoded:
Just to be clear, my personal issue with titanium is that when it's used structurally in the real world, it's almost always alloyed with another metal, often aluminum or steel. Pure titanium isn't especially hard or strong.

I guess it's possible that it's being used here solely for it's resistance to corrosion (which is considerable), I'm just weary of seeing it in SCP articles where what the author has in mind is super-strong, super-tough metal, which pure titanium really isn't.

FWIW, this is both a nitpick and a pet peeve of mine. I didn't hold it against the article, it just made me go "Ugh…" I still love the everything else about this piece. =]

:edit: werds r hadr

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation already has a moonbase, substantial space assets (at least tactical and strategic satellites plus advanced enough facilities and craft to intercept a microbus on it's way to Alpha Centauri) and highly reliable cargo-capable transport to and from a number of extra-solar planets. All setting up a base would require is repeat trips and time.

And that's the present.

item.title: Re: What if...?
item.content:encoded:
It's not that this is badly written- I think the documentation is done very well, outside of some excessive [redacted] and black boxes.

My only qualm is that this SCP, which goes so far outside the norm, is the perfect setup for really good tales. I think the supplement at the end could be expanded into a whole canon by itself, honestly. I think people are being excessively critical on this article when all I think they want is more on what happens after Nevada.

item.title: What if...?
item.content:encoded:
This article is about the objects they found on ILYENA, SCP-1124. There are some snippets in the documentation to help give the person reading a broader idea about the nature of the thing, but I don't believe any further knowledge of the thing was necessary in canon for someone only responsible for containing the object.

I don't elaborate on the other ideas for a few reasons.

  1. Honestly, at the time of my writing this, I was hardly capable of elaborating on this in an effective way.
  2. That's just me. I like to let the reader fill in the blanks. There is nothing here that says they're 682 eggs. My IMHC in the comment thread isn't what you're supposed to think.
  3. This was a conceptual article, /"what if…"/ I wanted to throw an idea at the wiki that I had not seen before here. The Foundation in the future, the Foundation in space.

I think Moose posted this mostly in relation to the 2000 contest. I don't think it was posted only because he personally thought it was good. There's not much here themed like this outside of tales - if anything, It's just an example of what they want to see for the contest but written in a better hand.

So, to anyone who disliked this: if you think any of these ideas have merit, do it better. I'll read it and probably enjoy it.

item.title: Admin Post
item.content:encoded: Your post here is HIGHLY inappropriate. Personal insults are not done, mate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, no. I don't get it, and I'm sort of pissed off that I wasted my time.

Thanks, dick.

Unless I'm reading this very wrongly, your post is kind of inappropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"So. Let's talk about ILYENA. And Tantalus. Let's talk about the continent the Foundation lost. About the Foundation's missing years…"

Why is this the lead in? I understand that we get extremely vague references to 682's rampage during the supplementation, but saying "we lost a continent" isn't talking about the continent the Foundation lost, or the even the years of fighting.

Besides that, I can't help but feel that the author took a very large amount of liberties when making this without ever really paying it back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, no. I don't get it, and I'm sort of pissed off that I wasted my time.

My thoughts while reading:
I think at one point, with the chart, it was like… I don't know, another planet? Alien life? Potential interest? Yeah, ok, follow that, make me want to know what's happening- oh, no, you're off doing your own thing, well let's see where that thing goes… I'm reading, I'm reading… goes straight to hell. Thanks, dick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
basically takes a crowbar to my head canon

Yes, but that's author commentary. One thing I love about the article is how readily it lends itself to a world of interpretations, even though the author's interpretation is very, very specific. To me that says it was written rather expertly. Something to strive for. And that helps balance out any awkwardness elsewhere. For me, at least!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really interested in where you're seeing that they lost time… because that's unintentional.

Oh, I know. The lost time is just one way that this could conceivably take place in the present day, since the Related Documentation says thirty years pass, then the continent is lost, then "SCP-███ went uncontained for ██ years." That is a lot of time.

Sure, it could still fit without missing time, but: it's also traditional to attempt to come up with an expanded interpretation for the blurb, which I wrote — I'm not always good at coming up with those, but I liked this one. ;)

(For instance, Troy featured Recovered Data File and expanded it to include Light being a current Site Director, and implying a current story with someone trying to find out more information on Light & major things that the Foundation has hidden. At the very least those shenanigans can add more mystery to a skip!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's see.

The writing is muddled, unclear, and offends most of the standards I try to hold articles. It still reads like a first draft.

The idea of a future article is cool. The idea of 682 eggs, humans being the only remaining salient race, and 682 being a "failed experiment" basically takes a crowbar to my head canon and is just… not good. It strikes me as the kind of deviant arty fan fix we always laugh at.

I can appreciate the article for what it tries to do, but I hate how it does it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember reading this before it was cool featured, but I don't really remember the Related documentation. And now I'm horny. So horny. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Saying it's 682 eggs actually makes me not like it. Don't like the future thing, either. Idk, it's just a bunch of little stuff making me not upvote. At the same time, I won't downvote.

One other thing (and probably also keeping me from voting at all) is Ilyena… Lews Therin Telamon misses her. I just can't get essentially the entire prologue to The Eye of the World out of my head every time I see the name in the article, so I get distracted and can't focus on the thing as a whole every time it pops up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I apparently never voted or commented on this. I darn well remember it. I don't think it had any effect on me the first time I saw it, and it doesn't now. Except now I'm considering a downvote because of the 682 connection. I don't remember that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never been quite sure what to think of this one. I like it at some points, but something seems just off at others. Faminepulse's explanation makes it interesting, to say the least, but the connection to 682 never really did much for me. Okay, so these things are related to everyone's favorite Big Friggin' Lizard, but that kind of takes away from it for me. here, it's scary that someone wanted to intentionally destroy every single sentient being in the galaxy. okay, that's some serious Forerunner Halo stuff right there. I can deal with that. I'm used to enough space operas that deal with that kind of stuff (currently reading The Reality Dysfunction, used to play 40K all the time), so, okay, not too much of a stretch. but related to 682? no thanks. My head canon for 682 was that it was always just a freak Lizard spawned from Nature's equivalent to 4chan-nasty, mean, brutish, and no one is exactly sure where the hell it came from (or really wants to know, for that matter). Basically, awesome article, but the 682 connection won't ever be part of my head canon.

One thing that's got me confused is the Grey Dragon incident-how did stuff landing in Nevada wreck Antarctica? could I get a little clarification on that timeline?

(although, for the longest time, i thought they destroyed Australia. not sure why, upon re-reading, but it might have something to do with my inherent hatred for half the animals what live down there. that place is scary, fauna-wise)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The canisters are egg shaped, no markings, smooth. They have lids on their ends that detach and reattach given a gentle pull. There are no adhesives, no screw tops or anything like that. They just come off and on.

FUCKING MAGNETS, HOW DO THEY WORK

Other than that and unalloyed titanium in the containment, I dig this, even with the Ay-Leen signifiers. I'm not buying it as 682 eggs, but it's not* being sold as 682 eggs, so, s'all good. =]

+1

:edit:

*explicitly

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He demonstrably does not hate everything. He finds humans revolting to the point of extreme hostility, but that's just because we're disgusting…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really interested in where you're seeing that they lost time… because that's unintentional.

item.title: My second article...
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit I squealed when I saw it was on the front page.

More spoilers for old time sake.
– hide block

IMHC

Ah… Titanium. Titanium is corrosion resistant, or so I know. I think it was the only thing I could find as a suitable containment material.

The continent is Antarctica… yeah I know.

The type of space-faring capability in the time of this canon is accessible to civilians. The Foundation is just slightly more advanced.

This was designed to eradicate sapient life from the universe. We are the last remaining. The creator of it and the civilization he belonged to aren't around anymore.

682 is an early, failed experiment that was dumped on Earth way back.

The audio is a train (nightmare retardant). The screaming is me (edited slightly).

This is inspired by a song called One Armed Scissor by At the Drive-In… The Mars Volta and theirselves use a lot of sci-fi and occult stuff in their music that makes for really good skip fuel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, yes. Good old 682 breeding grounds. Loved the execution and I'm glad to see it featured. A great piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh, you featured my suggestion. :)

If I could upvote again, I would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It grew on me pretty hard too (it doesn't really remind me of Aliens anymore either).

I think my favorite parts are stuff I didn't even notice the first time around, like the destroyed/lost continent, the implication that either this is in the future or the Foundation has lost decades of time or both, and other marvelous stuff like that.

Also I really like stories in which the Foundation is massively capable, with magitech and moon bases and cover-up ability like nothing else, but is still dwarfed by the enormity of what's out there, whether sharing Earth or simply casting eyes on our planet in passing.

If this weren't an already posted article, IMO it would be a great entry to the 2000 contest. I think it's heavily underrated. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember that I used to hate this article. Then it grew on me, so it got my upvote. Now, for some strange reason, I'm ecstatic about it being featured.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read this before I joined and it scared the hell out of me as it began to dawn on me what those things were. And I completely forgot about this until it got featured. Even just rereading it now, it kinda gives me the creeps.

It's not just that they're 682 eggs and that they're just about as hard to kill as the original…it's that that means there's probably more than one 682. That and the fact that they're all different. Biological matter would die quickly if exposed to ILEYNA's atmosphere. So these things aren't biological. They've adapted differently than the original, becoming different (for the most part) than the mostly-reptilian appearance of 682. These things are spiders, squids, hominids, and everything in between.

+1'd with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think having a base on another planet is too far-fetched budget wise, even by the SCP Foundation's standards. While I might believe that a containment site on a space station, or with good funding, the moon would be possible.

But the idea of 682 eggs is kind of cool. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a note: It's not necessarily set in the future. It's now ambiguous.

Yes, there's an inhospitable continent in the article, which could indicate the future. But there are more things on heaven on earth, Horatio, than are dreamt of in your philosophy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmm… I don't know how I feel about the Future Foundation.

To me, having SCPs in our own time makes humanity seem a little more outclassed when dealing with the unknown. If its almost or even more than a hundred years in the future, I'm always just wating for somebody to get the a deus-ex-raygun and save the day.

But that might just be because I've seen a lot of Star Trek lol.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is set in the future, actually. What with a whole continent becoming uninhabitable… which hasn't happened yet.

It used to explicitly be set in the 2200s, but really, between space technology ever on the up-and-up and the Foundation having access to some interesting stuff, the late 2000s could easily have them set up a base on another planet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is the present? Because if so when in gods name did ANYONE get a base on another planet? Downvote for the same reason I would downvote an SCP that was in the core of the Earth, it would have to come to us since we lack the ability or tech to go find it.

If I am just missing something here, please make me eat my words! Normally space SCPs are my faves but this one doesn't do it for shear lack of realism, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty okay with This. It's complex, and could use some touch-up work to solidify the underlying narrative (i.e. further separating the Ilyena & earth parts. Solid description separating -C from the Ilyena ones, etc.), but the narrative works.

I actually don't mind the furute prt of this. Why should only the past be subject to analysis, especially given that we have a developing scp time squad? Besides, the Xenomorphs would totally be held by the foundation. And the recent movies would be better for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would like to point out three caveats for this theory (however likely it may be).

  1. This article would have to be decommissioned, and all of its contents relocated to the page for the creature in question.
  2. Due to the interactions with SCP 343 and several tales written by DrBright, there would be quite a bit of, shall I say, "stretch" involved in the adaptation. This would include an extradimensional origin, and something about what Dr. Clef wrote to Dr. Bright.
  3. These things are metallic. That [EXPLETIVE REDACTED] lizard is not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, that's another problem I have with this. It's metalic 682. We really only need one of those.

Also:

Each is otherwise non-responsive to external stimuli save for physical contact with biological matter, which will cause instances of SCP-1124 to activate and [DATA EXPUNGED]. Reaction to biological matter originating from a sapient creature tends to elicit a more violent response.

Terrible expungement. Only 447 and 579 have pulled that sort of thing off, and I personally hate 579.

Don't get me wrong, I think that metallic aliens are something skip worthy as much as the next guy, but you've got to do it better.

EDIT: As my headcannon has developed, this skip has grown on me. Take your goddamn upvote.
